name: Every Drop Counts
description:
11am – 12:30pm | Poetry Reading | A Well is Born (8-11) 
with Radhika Chadha 
An engineer knows how to build the best well. But the farmer knows where to find the water! 
In this dramatic poetry reading, author Radhika Chadha brings to life a world where water is precious, ancient knowledge runs deep, and every drop of both deserves protection. 
A creative, moving plea for the things that sustain us. 
3pm – 4pm | Film Screening | WATERSCHOOL (For Ages 6-14) 
by WELM Foundation and Project WET 
Water sustains everything. Who sustains the water?  
WATERSCHOOL follows students living along the Amazon, Mississippi, Danube, Nile, Ganges, and Yangtze as they learn what it takes to protect the water that sustains their communities. Supported by local partners including WWF India, the Swarovski Waterschool programme has been connecting children, families, and communities around water since 2000. 
A documentary about the next generation, made for the next generation. 
Around the World in 50 Sundays is a journey that begins, not with a ticket, but with opening a book. In a city like Bangalore where families are always in motion, the BIC Library hopes to offer stillness and a quiet, welcoming space for both parents and children where reading becomes a shared way of being and not a task. 
Every Sunday, we open our doors to invite you into a library that listens; building a rhythm of return, a habit of joy and a new community bound not just by books, but by the weekly ritual of reading together. 
We welcome your ideas and input. If you have suggestions for children’s events or activities that align with this vision, we’d love to hear from you. Please reach out to us at library@bangaloreinternationalcentre.org 
Supported by:

location.name: Bangalore International Centre, 7, 4th Main Road, Domlur II Stage, Bangalore, Karnataka, 560071, India

name: The Zone
description:
Freedom of expression is a constitutional right.  
But codified freedom and social permission are not the same thing.  
This evening by Myān Dance Company unfolds in six segments, each tracing a different pressure point in the life of a working artist. The need to be seen. The fatigue of hustle culture. Stolen moments of relief. The weight of expectation, the pull of impulsive choices, and the complicated fallout of cancel culture. 
The evening includes an improvisational segment. The audience is asked a question. One response is chosen at random. One dancer interprets it, unrehearsed, in movement. 
What you accept, and what you don’t, is also part of the performance. The stage is only as free as the room allows. 





Cast:
Mukta Nagpal
Ashish Rao
Jyoti Naik
Sasha Shetty
Archana Gokhale
Antra Shankar 



Supported by:

location.name: Bangalore International Centre, 7, 4th Main Road, Domlur II Stage, Bangalore, Karnataka, 560071, India

name: Rewind / Rebound
description:
Memory is not a recording. It is a negotiation. 
Rewind / Rebound is a trio performance that treats time as something that can be reversed, reconstructed, and remade. What is remembered is never quite what happened. What is lost generates something new. 
The performance unfolds in three phases: expression, rewind, and rebound. What begins as improvisation is reconstructed in reverse, and what cannot be perfectly recalled becomes the material for what comes next. Each mistake becomes choreography. Each return reshapes what came before.  
Three performers. Three phases. Three histories. 
Supported by:

location.name: Bangalore International Centre, 7, 4th Main Road, Domlur II Stage, Bangalore, Karnataka, 560071, India

name: Paper Trail
description:
One instrument. One evening. Centuries of music.  
In this intimate concert, Antara Dasgupta will trace the sounds of classical guitar across time and geography. Drawing on etchings by Francisco Goya, poems by Wilhelm Müller set to music by Franz Schubert, and Alice in Wonderland, she uncovers the conversations between music and other expressions of art.  
Melodies that echo through time, resounding still, today.  
Supported by:

location.name: 7, 4th Main Road, Domlur II Stage, Bengaluru, Karnataka, 560 071, India

name: Sur, Parampara, and Ritu
description:
A raga is not simply performed, it is experienced. 
This concert presents select evening ragas through the lens of two distinct Hindustani traditions. The Kirana gharana unfolds in slow, meditative elaboration. The Gwalior gharana brings structure, clarity, and rhythmic vitality. Together, they frame the unfolding of a raga.  
Muddu Mohan on vocals, Vyasmurti Katti on harmonium, and Keshav Joshi on tabla trace the raga from its quietest beginnings to its fullest expression. The Ritu Ragas speak directly to you. The feeling of the changing seasons, expressed, heard and understood.  
Be stilled. Be seen. 
Supported by:

location.name: Bangalore International Centre, 7, 4th Main Road, Domlur II Stage, Bangalore, Karnataka, 560071, India

name: Enam
description:
Maricha knows how this ends. 
Cursed and exiled, he transforms into a golden deer to deceive Sita and draw Rama into the forest. The trap works. It also destroys him. 
Enam, Malayalam for deer, traces the arc of a character usually reduced to plot device. What does it feel like to be the bait? To know your role, hate it, and play it anyway? 
Karthik Manikandan choreographs and performs this solo Bharatanatyam production, pulling Maricha out of the Ramayana’s margins. The demon. The deer. The destiny he cannot escape. 
Supported by:

location.name: Bangalore International Centre, 7, 4th Main Road, Domlur II Stage, Bangalore, Karnataka, 560071, India

name: Bookmarked
description:
Bring a book by a woman author and settle in! 
Whether it’s a novel you’ve been meaning to finish, a poetry collection, or a memoir gathering dust, this session is yours. A shared space to slow down and give women’s writing the deep attention it deserves. We’ll read together in companionable quiet, and close with an optional chance to share a line, a title, or a thought. 
And if your TBR pile is looking sparse, Atta Galatta will have a selection of books by women authors ready for you to pick up and dive into. 
This event is part of our Programmes at the Library series at BIC. 
We’re reimagining the BIC Library as a warm, welcoming space for connection and exploration. Through thoughtfully curated sessions, we aim to engage the diverse interests and ages within our community. Our goal is to create an enriching environment for discovery, experimentation, and collaboration, beyond the books on our shelves! 
We welcome your ideas and input. If you have ideas for events or activities that align with our vision, we’d be delighted to hear from you. Please reach out to us at library@bangaloreinternationalcentre.org 
In collaboration with:

location.name: Bangalore International Centre, 7, 4th Main Road, Domlur II Stage, Bangalore, Karnataka, 560071, India

name: Has Trump 2.0 Commandeered India’s Foreign Policy?
description:
When the world shifts, what anchors a nation’s choices? 
India’s Tryst With the World: A Foreign Policy Manifesto arrives at a moment of visible churn in India’s external relations. With shifts in United States policy under a second Trump presidency, long-assumed alignments have grown uncertain; prompting rapid recalibrations toward China, renewed invocations of friendship with Russia, and fresh questions about strategic autonomy. The book frames this moment not as disruption alone, but as an opportunity to rethink how India engages the world. 
Co-edited by Salman Khurshid and Salil Shetty, the volume sets the stage for a wide-ranging conversation on diplomacy, values, and global responsibility. With Nirupama Rao and Suhasini Haider in conversation and Salil Shetty moderating, the evening reflects on India’s foreign policy record and its evolving role in a post-Trump international order, inviting careful listening, debate, and reflection as the contours of the future come into view. 
Bring your questions!

location.name: Bangalore International Centre, 7, 4th Main Road, Domlur II Stage, Bangalore, Karnataka, 560071, India

name: I, Poppy
description:
80 minutes | Mewadi and Hindi with English subtitles | India, France | 2025 
Black Gold. Medicine and livelihood. Illegal and industrialised.  
Vardibai farms poppies in rural Rajasthan. Her son, Mangilal, organizes protests against the Narcotics Bureau officers who issue licences, fix crop prices, and revoke permissions when farmers resist. She tends her fields. He mobilizes crowds. She pays bribes to survive. He fights the system that demands them. 
Shot over four years, I, Poppy watches a Dalit family caught between two impossible choices: comply and survive, or resist and risk everything. When Mangilal wins, Vardibai loses her licence. Then the rains come. 
Filmmaker Vivek Chaudhary joins virtually for a conversation with Sunanda Bhat of Vikalp Bengaluru, followed by a Q&A with the audience. 
In collaboration with:

location.name: Bangalore International Centre, 7, 4th Main Road, Domlur II Stage, Bangalore, Karnataka, 560071, India

name: Bangladesh: Humiliation, Carnage, Liberation, Chaos
description:
December 1971, Bangladesh. 
For the first time, a liberated state. No longer East Pakistan. A hard-won independence, a people pulled through civil war, a refugee crisis, the guerrilla campaigns of the Mukti Bahini, and the military engagement of the Indian Armed Forces. 
Through recordings of officers who fought in the war, archival footage, and maps, Subroto Chattopadhyay and Anil P Dere bring the reach and complexity of this history to life. The presentation draws on years of research by The Peninsula Studios into one of the most consequential geopolitical events of the twentieth century. 
The session will open to the audience for questions.

location.name: Bangalore International Centre, 7, 4th Main Road, Domlur II Stage, Bangalore, Karnataka, 560071, India

name: The Ravi Iyer Fusion Experience
description:
Ragas and rock have more in common than you think.  
This concert is inspired by the idea that different musical traditions can have a conversation rather than exist separately. Classical Hindustani and Carnatic ragas find new breath in this concert.  
Ravi Iyer on guitar, Anirudha S Bhat on mridangam, and Rahul Pophali on tabla, bring the raw energy of rock, the smooth touch of blues, and the improvisational spirit of jazz into dialogue. 
Traditions bridged. Melodies reconstructed. 
Supported by:

location.name: Bangalore International Centre, 7, 4th Main Road, Domlur II Stage, Bangalore, Karnataka, 560071, India
